article_title: 5 Proxmox services that turn your PC into a self-hosted powerhouse
core_thesis: Proxmox is a versatile virtualization platform that allows users to run multiple isolated services on a single machine. The article recommends five high-impact self-hosted services—Home Assistant, Plex, Pi-hole, Immich, and Nextcloud—that can transform a PC into a powerful home server, emphasizing ease of setup, cost savings, and enhanced control over data and privacy.
detailed_summary: The article introduces Proxmox as a virtualization platform for running multiple services in containers or VMs. It then details five services: Home Assistant for advanced smart home automation (running HAOS in a VM for add-on support); Plex Media Server for self-hosted streaming (with local and remote access options, noting Jellyfin as a free alternative); Pi-hole for network-wide ad blocking via DNS filtering; Immich for self-hosted photo backup (with remote access via VPN, Cloudflare Tunnel, or reverse proxy); and Nextcloud as a general-purpose cloud storage replacement for Google Drive. The article stresses the importance of backups and warns about running untrusted scripts. It also mentions useful scripts for quick setup but advises caution.

entities.name: Plex
entities.role: Media server software for self-hosted streaming

entities.name: Immich
entities.role: Self-hosted photo and video backup service

entities.name: Nextcloud

entities.name: Jellyfin

key_data_points: Proxmox allows running multiple isolated services on one machine using containers or VMs, ideal for homelab consolidation (🟢 relevant to homelabbing: reduces hardware costs and power usage).
Home Assistant OS (HAOS) in a VM provides access to add-ons, unlike the container installation (🟢 relevant to smart home enthusiasts: enables advanced automations and integrations).
Plex requires a subscription for remote access, but Jellyfin offers free remote streaming (🟢 relevant to cost-conscious users: Jellyfin is a viable alternative).
Pi-hole blocks ads network-wide by acting as a DNS server and returning blank responses for known ad servers (🟢 relevant to privacy: reduces tracking and bandwidth usage).
Immich provides self-hosted photo backup with multi-user support and remote access via VPN or reverse proxy (🟢 relevant to data control: avoids cloud subscription fees).

Nextcloud replaces Google Drive/Dropbox with self-hosted file storage, requiring robust backup to prevent data loss (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: can be used to host exfiltration targets or test backup security).
The article warns against running untrusted scripts, emphasizing security hygiene (🟢 relevant to cybersecurity: reinforces safe scripting practices).
methodology_steps: Install Proxmox on a dedicated PC or server.
Create a VM for Home Assistant OS (HAOS) to leverage add-ons for smart home automation.
Set up a Plex Media Server in a container or VM, mount a separate drive for media storage, and configure local/remote access (consider Jellyfin for free remote streaming).
Deploy Pi-hole in a lightweight container, configure it as the network's DNS server to block ads across all devices.
Install Immich in a container for self-hosted photo backup; enable remote access via VPN, Cloudflare Tunnel, or reverse proxy.
Set up Nextcloud in a container for file sync and sharing; implement a robust backup strategy (e.g., 3-2-1 rule) to protect against hardware failure.
conclusions: Proxmox is an excellent platform for hosting multiple self-hosted services, offering isolation and resource efficiency. The five services listed provide significant value: Home Assistant for smart home control, Plex/Jellyfin for media streaming, Pi-hole for ad blocking, Immich for photo backup, and Nextcloud for file storage. Each service enhances privacy, reduces reliance on cloud subscriptions, and gives users full control over their data. However, proper backup and security measures are essential to avoid data loss and unauthorized access.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is highly relevant to your homelab and self-hosting interests, providing a curated list of services that can be deployed on Proxmox to create a powerful home server. As a programmer and cybersecurity enthusiast, you can leverage these services to build a test environment for Red Teaming exercises (e.g., Nextcloud as a target for data exfiltration, Pi-hole for network monitoring). The article also offers practical advice on remote access and backups, which aligns with your DevOps and security needs. Additionally, as a student, you can use Nextcloud for file sync and Immich for photo backup, saving money on cloud subscriptions.
extra_advice: Consider setting up a dedicated Proxmox host with these services as a lab environment. For Red Teaming, intentionally misconfigure Nextcloud or Immich to practice exploiting weak access controls or backup failures. Also, use Pi-hole logs to analyze DNS traffic patterns—useful for network reconnaissance exercises.
